content.full_text:
(916) 322·5662 
Mr. John I. Mahan 
1212 Los Padres Blvd. 
Santa Clara, CA 
95050 
Dear Mr. Mahan: 
Administration 
322·5660 
Executive/legal 
322·5901 
Enforcement 
322-6441 
December 6, 1983 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice; Our 
Advice No. A-83/259 
Thank you for your letter requesting information about the 
Political Reform Act. 
Specifically, you asked whether a 
particular political advertisement, paid for by your opponent, 
satisfies the requirements of the Political Reform Act. 
Because 
you are asking about the activities of a person other than 
yourself, I can only provide you with general, rather than 
specific, advice. 
The provisions of the Political Reform Act related to 
political advertisements require: 
1. 
That contributions and expenditures for such 
advertisements be reported (Government Code Sections 
84200-842171/), and 
2. 
That, in the case of mass mailings, the sender must 
show, on the outside of each envelope and on at least one of 
the inserts included within each piece of mail, the sender's 
name, street address and city in no less than six-point type 
(Section 84305) • 
The Act does not contain any provisions regulating the 
content of advertisements, nor does it regulate the format of 
newspaper advertisements. 
1/ Hereinafter all statutory references are to the 
Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 


Mr. John I. Mahan 
December 6, 1983 
Page Two 
Thank you again for consulting us on this matter. 
Please 
remember that my advice is confined solely to the provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (Section 81000, ~ 
~.). 
Very truly yours, 
2~~'XB6Il/ 
Janis Shank McLean 
Counsel, Legal Division 
JSM: km

sections.question: whether a particular political advertisement, paid for by your opponent, satisfies the requirements of the Political Reform Act
sections.conclusion: The provisions of the Political Reform Act related to political advertisements require: 1. That contributions and expenditures for such advertisements be reported (Government Code Sections 84200-84217), and 2. That, in the case of mass mailings, the sender must show, on the outside of each envelope and on at least one of the inserts included within each piece of mail, the sender's name, street address and city in no less than six-point type (Section 84305). The Act does not contain any provisions regulating the content of advertisements, nor does it regulate the format of newspaper advertisements.
sections.question_synthetic: Does a particular political advertisement paid for by the requester's opponent comply with the Political Reform Act's requirements?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Political Reform Act requires reporting of contributions and expenditures for political advertisements and, for mass mailings, identification of the sender with name, street address, and city in at least six-point type. The Act does not regulate advertisement content or format for newspaper advertisements.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Standard advice letter format with clear question and conclusion sections. Document identifies itself as 'Advice No. A-83/259'. Advice limited to general guidance rather than specific advice regarding another person's activities.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: whether a particular political advertisement, paid for by your opponent, satisfies the requirements of the Political Reform Act

CONCLUSION: The provisions of the Political Reform Act related to political advertisements require: 1. That contributions and expenditures for such advertisements be reported (Government Code Sections 84200-84217), and 2. That, in the case of mass mailings, the sender must show, on the outside of each envelope and on at least one of the inserts included within each piece of mail, the sender's name, street address and city in no less than six-point type (Section 84305). The Act does not contain any provisions regulating the content of advertisements, nor does it regulate the format of newspaper advertisements.

embedding.summary: Mr. Mahan requested advice on whether an opponent's political advertisement complies with the Political Reform Act. The FPPC provided general guidance on disclosure and identification requirements for political advertisements under the Act.
